speaker: Speaker 1
text: Welcome to the documentary from the BBC World Service.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Together we host Good Bad Billionaire, the podcast that looks at how some of the richest people on the planet made their money. You can find us every week wherever you get your BBC podcasts.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: We've covered some of the biggest names in tech, sport, entertainment and finance from Elon Musk to Ronnie Screwalla, Tyler Perry to Michael O'Leary and even Selena Gomez and Arnold Schwarzenegger. In this episode we cover one of the newly minted billionaires on the list, Queen Bey herself, Beyonce.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: We hope you enjoy listening. Yes, Beyonce Knowles was born in 1981 in Houston, Texas in the USA. Her father Matthew worked at Xerox selling medical equipment. Her mother Tina owned a hair salon. And according to Matthew, made them their first million dollars before Beyonce was even born. And she said, I went to private school, we had a very nice house, we had cars, we had a housekeeper. You can actually Google her family home, it's pretty nice, grand looking white house, 5,000 square feet, four bedrooms, three bathrooms and a swimming pool, so nice.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So Beyonce grew up comfortable and everyone describes her when she was growing up as someone who was quiet, who was shy. Her parents actually put Beyonce in a dance class because she was too shy. And the story goes is that when Beyonce was seven, her dance teacher, woman called Dallet, noticed that she could sing and said, it blew me away. I told her to sing again and because she was very shy, I promised her a dollar and she sang it again. Interesting incentive for Beyonce there. And Dallet then entered Beyonce into a talent show and when she got up, she absolutely commanded the stage. And her mum Tina, who will come up again in the story, says it was something that happened on that stage I had never seen before. Beyonce won first class, she took home a trophy, $100, so that was the beginning of her journey.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Isn't it funny because I remember one of our other billionaires, the minute they took to the stage, somebody saw something, Rihanna, remember that one? Exactly. So from then on, singing, dancing became part of her life. According to Dallet, Beyonce's parents worked pretty hard developing her. They got her a vocal coach. Matthew and Tina's permission, Dallet says she was dragging her all over town to dance competitions and pageants and stuff like that. Age nine, she moved schools to Parker Elementary, which is kind of specialized in music and dance. And her younger sister Solange said, Beyonce practiced her bedroom over and over and over again. And whenever everyone said, 'Okay, that's good, we've got it,' she'd say, 'No, I want to do it again.' So very driven, perfectionism. Exactly. So you might ask yourself, where did this work ethic come from? Beyonce credits her parents. She said, both were entrepreneurs. I watched them struggle, working 18-hour days. They taught me that nothing worth having comes easily. And in particular, her dad, Matthew Knowles, stressed discipline. He was really tough on her. But Beyonce herself said, I wasn't doing this because I didn't have a choice or to support the family, important for child entertainers, or because I had to get out of a bad situation. I was just determined, this is what I wanted to do so badly. Okay. So she was making a name for herself in the local community, winning competitions. One in her salon to local mothers, Diane and Deborah approached the Knowles and said, 'Listen, we want to form a young version of N Vogue.' 1990. We're out here. Groups were all the bigger at the time. There was a gap in the market for a younger version of that. So Beyonce became the first member of a band called Girls Tyme, spelled T Y M E, Girls Tyme. We put a team in place, a financial backer, producers, songwriter. They tried 30 girls. Six were finally selected for the band. They had intense rehearsals, they practiced nearly every day for months at their family home. Beyonce's family home, the girls did a regular boot camp as per our opening scene. You remember running down the river by the bayou. And they traveled to the West Coast to record their first record, but tensions were already showing. So those involved have said that Beyonce's dad, Matthew, was trying to position her as the lead, although Matthew, obviously disputes this portrayal of himself. It's worth noting that there were a lot of people involved at this point, all of whom were developing Girls Tyme for months on end. That's not particularly unusual when it comes to girl bands or boy bands. It's estimated that back then this would have cost around $100,000 because it is expensive, you're working with a lot of people. So the band needed to sign a deal, but their producer was shopping the record around major labels for about a year. They had no success. And their last hope was a popular TV talent show called Star Search, Search, quite similar to, I guess X Factor, Pop Idol. Yeah, like an early version of that. At the time, it was the largest national talent show. Child stars have been on Star Search including some big names actually, Britney Spears, Christina Aguilera. They practiced for over a month, they got tight, they got polished. They were up against an adult male rock band called Skeleton Crew. I can sort of picture that band. And Beyonce said, I had not even thought about imagining losing as a possibility. But lose they did. And this whole process of losing Star Search caused also a deep rift within the band. Ashley left, soon the producer's songwriter also parted ways. And this left the stage clear for Matthew Knowles. He quit his job to manage the band full time. This is a guy who's probably high up and he just goes, I'm just going to quit to manage my daughter's band. Well, they must have been absolutely convinced that they had a star on their hands because as you say, it's not like this was their only hope. They both had good jobs. And to pack it in, particularly after losing, you know, at that point, you'd say, oh well, nice try. It's not going to be, but they doubled down on this. So more girls left, they brought another one in, the band changed its name. And one former member Latavia said, Matthew did not mince his words. She said, and it can be tough to take that kind of criticism when you are a little girl. We would try not to let it break us. Shades of like the Jackson 5 almost in this, isn't it? But a high pressure environment. Definitely, especially for young girls, right? And also it's worth noting things were not going very well for Beyonce at this point. So the Knowles were also having financial problems because as we said, their dad quit his job to manage them. The family had to move out of that lovely house, the parents even split up for six months. Though, you know, in personal stakes, Beyonce's life was not going particularly well.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But then came one of those moments in our stories which basically changed everything. And what's in a name? Well, quite a lot because this band changes its name to Destiny's Child and Destiny beckoned. Very good. Yeah. Four years earlier, Beyonce had met an A&R, that's artist from repertoire, they're the kind of people responsible for finding talent called Teresa La Barbera. White. Teresa was keen on her, but couldn't convince her label to sign the act. Fast forward to 1996, Beyonce's now 14. Teresa has got a new job at Columbia Records, it's part of Sony. So Matthew calls her up and this time she does manage to convince the label and Destiny's Child is in fact her first signing. Her first single? No, no, no, which is an incredible song, by the way, major hit, peaking at number three on the US Billboard chart. But the album only made number 67. Now, this failure probably was a blessing in a way. Beyonce has said, the label didn't really believe we were pop stars. They underestimated us and because of that, they allowed us to write our own songs, write our own video treatments. It ended up being the best thing because that's when I became an artist and I took control. That's fascinating, isn't it? So they didn't think they were going to be a big enough deal to micromanage them, so they let them kind of do their, do their own thing. And the album thing is interesting because in the mid 90s, late 90s, album sales were still where a lot of the money comes from. It's huge. It's not like now you get one stream or two streams. Album sales were kind of like the bulk of your earnings.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Well, in 1999, Destiny's Child took that artistic freedom and they brought out an album which became critically acclaimed. Something called The Writings on the Wall. Do you know any songs from this era? I think Say My Name is on this. Ah, there we go. Say My Name, Jumping, Jumping, Bills, Bills, Bills. Hugely influential pop songs, which actually great music videos that still stand up. Yeah, like 8 million copies, three number one singles. But often with these kind of, you know, crafted pop bands, they were actually at war. In fact, they only found out that they'd been sacked when they watched the video for Say My Name and saw two new members lip syncing to their voices. Ouch. That's pretty brutal, isn't it? This is a bit like the guy who played Darth Vader, who didn't realize that his voice had been replaced by James Earl Jones until he actually saw the film. That's got to hurt. Um, anyway, but Matthew said the allegations are totally untrue. He also said that he owns the name Destiny's Child as a Texas corporation. And therefore, he could decide who the members are. Perhaps legally that's right. The lawsuit was settled out of court later that year. So by the third album, there were just three members of Destiny's Child. The ones we all know, Beyonce, Kelly, a new girl called Michelle. And their 2001 album Survivor was Beyonce's very first number one album. The title and the lyrics were actually about the band's turmoil. So there's a line in there that says, 'You thought I wouldn't sell without you, sued nine million.' Ooh. I always thought when I was growing up that that was about a nasty ex-boyfriend who didn't believe in Beyonce or the rest of the girls. But actually it's about all this internal struggle going on within the band at the time. And also by that point, by the way, there's no denying this was the Beyonce show. She was front and center, she was also getting major writing and production credits on the album, which we know from the Taylor Swift episode is so crucial to making money. Yeah. And Survivor, so sold 10 million, that's a lot. But it's always tricky to find out exactly how much money artists are making from their music.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: In 2016, entertainment website, The Jasmine Brand, reported on the terms of the original deal. Destiny's Child signed with Sony, apparently they got $85,000 advance for the first album. The second album they'd get between $350 and $700, similar amounts for third and so on. And their royalty rate was said at 28% for the first album, but the rates would rise if an album sold over 500,000 copies. However, and this is fairly standard, all the money that label paid to cover producer fees and promotion, marketing would be recouped by Sony from the album profits. So we don't know if the advances were split equally. Latavia and Latoya have claimed that they weren't. But I think it's hard to be at the stage, show of Beyonce had personally made a million. And in order to do that, Beyonce needs to go solo. So at the end of 2001, Destiny's Child announced a hiatus. She's only 21 at this stage, she's on her own. Beyonce releases a collaboration with a rapper, the man who would later on become her husband, Jay Z. And according to Beyonce, she wasn't flush with cash at the beginning of their relationship. She says, 'We used calling cards and Skype when we were falling in love. I couldn't afford the international hotel bills, so I would literally international calling cards to call him.' That's fascinating. Yes, expensive those phone calls from your hotel room. At this point, Beyonce started diversifying her revenue streams. That sounds all very businesslike. She started cashing in on sponsorships, she replaced Britney Spears as the face of Pepsi. She's dipping her toe into acting, she starred in an MTV made for TV film, Carmen, a hip-hopper, Crown. And then the big screen as Foxy Cleopatra in Austin Powers' Goldmember. I don't remember that. And she also did a song for it from memory. She did. The song is she's quite good, I think. But forgotten into the mists of time. Yeah. So things exploded in 2003. When she released her debut solo album, Dangerously in Love, a debut at number one in the States. It earned her five Grammy awards and it was a truly global hit. In fact, she became the first female artist in the fifth artist ever to simultaneously top both the singles charts and the album charts in the US and the UK. That's really, really difficult. And most importantly for Beyonce's journey to a billion, Dangerously in Love became one of the best selling albums of the 21st century. So between this, the acting jobs, the Pepsi gig, she was, she was selling, selling, selling. Yeah. So Survivor, sold 10 million, that's a lot. But it's always tricky to find out exactly how much money artists are making from their music.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: In 2016, Forbes estimated her earnings at $115 million. And just breaking that down a bit, you've got the album sales, her deal with Sony at the time was rumored to be structured more like a joint venture. You know, she got a royalty rate around 20% and then she was getting 50% endorsements with Pepsi and she was making big money from her fragrances, Heat, Pulse. They were making tens of millions a year. But heat has actually got seven spin-off scents. And, you know, it was estimated that her fragrance alone would have made her $100 million. So all of that combined, Forbes named Beyonce the most powerful celebrity in the world that year based on the money she was earning and her fame. But she's only got about a quarter of a billion at this point. Peanuts. So let's fast forward to 2016 for her next big business move. She decided she's going to launch her own fashion line. She had actually tried this before in 2005, she launched a brand called House of Dereon with her mum Tina, which was kind of denim, skirts, dresses, started okay, but in the early 2010s, House of Dereon was quietly shuttered. But then in 2016, she started tapping into the growing athleisure market. Uh, with a line called Ivy Park. And if you want to hear more about athleisure, you can check out our episode on Ben Francis, the Gymshark guy. Yeah, that's interesting. I have to say, I have to admit, I was not one for the celebrity fragrance era. But I have to say, Ivy Park. It's a name, it's a brand that I've seen. It's a name that I recognise. It's a name that I think is quite powerful. Yeah. So, but you know, in 2018, Beyonce bought Topshop out of that partnership after Philip Green was named as having taken legal action to prevent the publication of allegations of sexual harassment of staff. Allegations by the way that he totally denied. Instead, in 2019, Ivy Park announced it was partnering with Adidas. And this is turning a bad situation into an opportunity because Adidas is the second richest sports company in the world. They offered her creative control and meanwhile, Arcadia group, which owned Topshop, was actually collapsing. So you know, it's not a surprise that that partnership broke up. But it's not doing too well, is it? Because Adidas is reportedly down more than 50% to around 40 million in 2022. Which is pretty bad for a business the size of Adidas. They projected $250 million in sales. Yeah. But Beyonce's Ivy Park has been losing money. And, you know, it's an interesting case study. But Beyonce, she decided to buy herself out of the Adidas deal in 2023, reportedly because she wanted more creative control and to retain ownership. So, you know, it's definitely a lesson in business and entrepreneurship. But also, you know, we can talk about that later. But also, you know, she's not a self-made woman. I think that's a lesson for all of us. She's not a self-made woman. She's a woman who's been supported by her family. She's been supported by her husband. She's been supported by her business partners. And she has also been supported by her fans. She's a woman who has been truly blessed. Thank you, Beyonce.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Well, let's give her scores. So for wealth, I'm going to give her a seven out of ten. She's a billionaire, but not quite in the top tier. For controversy, I'm going to give her a three out of ten. She's had a few bumps in the road, but nothing too scandalous. For power, I'm going to give her a nine out of ten. She's one of the most powerful women in the world. And for legacy, I'm going to give her a ten out of ten. She's changed the music industry forever.
